name: Fasil Ghebbi
shortDescription: A 17th-century royal enclosure with castles unlike anything else in sub-Saharan Africa.
description: Fasil Ghebbi — 'Royal Enclosure' — in Gondar, Ethiopia, contains six castles and numerous smaller structures built by Emperor Fasilides and his successors in the 17th–18th centuries. The architecture is a remarkable fusion of Aksumite, Indian, and Portuguese Baroque influences. Gondar became known as the 'Camelot of Africa' for its cluster of fairytale castles, which remain extraordinarily well-preserved.

name: Great Zimbabwe
shortDescription: The greatest stone structure in sub-Saharan Africa, built without mortar.
description: Great Zimbabwe was the capital of the Kingdom of Zimbabwe at its height in the 13th–15th centuries, when the city housed up to 18,000 people. Its massive dry-stone walls — built from perfectly fitted granite blocks without mortar — include the Great Enclosure with a wall 11 metres high and 5 metres thick. The site gave its name to the modern country and represents the peak of pre-colonial southern African civilisation.

name: Fort Jesus
shortDescription: A Portuguese star fortress on the Swahili Coast that changed hands nine times in 33 years.
description: Built by the Portuguese in 1593 to protect the port of Mombasa, Fort Jesus was designed by the Italian architect Giovanni Battista Cairati using the latest Renaissance military principles. The fort changed hands nine times in 33 years — between the Portuguese, the Swahili, the Omani Arabs, and the British — making its history a microcosm of East African colonial competition. Its shape echoes a human form when viewed from above.

name: Ksar Ouled Soltane
shortDescription: A rare multi-story Berber fortified granary (ghorfas) in perfect condition.
description: Ksar Ouled Soltane is one of the best-preserved ksour (Berber fortified granaries) in Tunisia. Its two interior courtyards are lined with ghorfas — vaulted storage rooms stacked three or four stories high — where nomadic Berber families stored grain and valuables. The lower courtyard dates to the 15th century; the upper to the early 20th century. The site famously appeared in George Lucas's Star Wars as Mos Espa slave quarters.

name: Elmina Castle
shortDescription: The oldest surviving European building south of the Sahara, a central site of the transatlantic slave trade.
description: Built by the Portuguese in 1482 as São Jorge da Mina ('St George of the Mine'), Elmina was the first European fort built in sub-Saharan Africa. Initially a trading post for gold, ivory, and pepper, it became the oldest and largest slave-holding post in sub-Saharan Africa after the Dutch captured it in 1637. Through its 'Door of No Return,' an estimated 30,000 enslaved people passed annually at the trade's height.

name: Kano City Walls
shortDescription: Ancient earthen walls encircling one of Africa's great medieval Islamic cities.
description: The walls of Kano are among the most extensive pre-colonial fortifications in sub-Saharan Africa. Originally built around 1095 by the Hausa king Gijimasu, they were expanded over centuries to eventually stretch over 14 miles, enclosing 1,700 hectares. Made of sun-dried brick (tubali), they rose up to 12 metres in places and were pierced by 15 gates. Much of the wall still stands today, making it one of Africa's finest earthen fortifications.

name: Sigiriya
shortDescription: The 'Lion Rock' — an extraordinary 5th-century palace-fortress atop a sheer 200m rock.
description: Sigiriya ('Lion Rock') is a UNESCO-listed ancient rock fortress and palace built by King Kasyapa I of Sri Lanka in the late 5th century. The king carved his palace into the top of a 200-metre granite monolith, accessible via a staircase that passed through the mouth and paws of a giant lion. Remarkable frescoes on the rock face, sophisticated water gardens, and the summit ruins make it one of the world's most extraordinary fortified sites.
